title: Unreal Insights, Profiling & Optimization
html:
You can't fix what you can't measure

By now you have a portfolio of games across many genres. Some of them probably stutter, drop frames, or hitch when too much is happening. The instinct of every beginner is to guess what's slow and start randomly optimizing. That is the single biggest waste of time in game development. Optimization is a measurement discipline: you profile first, find the real bottleneck, fix that one thing, then measure again. This lesson teaches you the tools and the three highest-impact techniques — LODs, pooling, and culling — that solve the vast majority of real performance problems.

Law of optimization.
Measure, fix the biggest bottleneck, measure again. Never optimize on a hunch. A change that 'feels faster' but isn't backed by numbers is a change you cannot defend.

CPU-bound vs GPU-bound

Every frame, the CPU prepares work (game logic, animation, physics, issuing draw calls) and the GPU renders it. Your frame time is roughly the max of the two, not the sum — they overlap. The first question is always: which one is the limit? Use the console command stat unit to find out.

console (~ key in PIE)
// Press the tilde (~) key while playing to open the console stat unit // shows Frame / Game / Draw / GPU / RHIT in ms stat fps // frames per second + frame ms stat gpu // per-pass GPU cost breakdown stat game // gameplay/tick cost breakdown on the CPU

Read stat unit like this: Frame is total time. Game is CPU gameplay (Tick, physics, AI). Draw is the CPU render thread cost (largely draw-call submission). GPU is the graphics card. Whichever of Game/Draw/GPU is closest to Frame is your bottleneck. At 60 FPS you have a 16.6 ms budget; at 30 FPS, 33.3 ms.

SymptomLikely causeLook at
Game highToo much Tick/AI/physicsstat game, Insights
Draw highToo many draw calls / actorsstat scenerendering
GPU highOverdraw, shaders, resolutionstat gpu, GPU Visualizer

Unreal Insights: the real profiler

stat commands give you a live overview. Unreal Insights is the deep, timeline-based profiler. You launch it alongside your game, capture a trace, then scrub through every frame to see exactly which function ate the time. Enable tracing from the editor toolbar dropdown (the little arrow next to the play/profile icons) or from the command line.

cmd — launch with tracing
# Launch your packaged build or editor with trace channels enabled MyGame.exe -trace=cpu,gpu,frame,bookmark -statnamedevents # Then open the recorded .utrace in Unreal Insights: UnrealInsights.exe

In Insights, the Timing Insights view shows a flame-graph per frame on the Game thread and Render thread. Find a slow frame (a tall spike in the frames track), zoom in, and read the widest bars — those are your costliest functions. You can add custom scopes in C++ so your own systems show up by name:

EnemyManager.cpp
void AEnemyManager::UpdateAllEnemies(float DeltaTime) { // Named scope — appears as a labeled bar in Unreal Insights TRACE_CPUPROFILER_EVENT_SCOPE(EnemyManager_UpdateAll); for (AEnemy* Enemy : ActiveEnemies) { if (Enemy) Enemy->TickBehavior(DeltaTime); } }

Technique 1 — LODs (Levels of Detail)

A character mesh that's 80,000 triangles up close has no business rendering 80,000 triangles when it's a 12-pixel dot on the horizon. LODs are progressively simpler versions of a mesh that swap in based on screen size. UE5 can auto-generate them. Select a Static Mesh, open the Mesh Editor, set Number of LODs, and enable Auto Compute LOD Distances. For Nanite meshes, LODs are handled automatically by the renderer — but skeletal meshes, foliage, and non-Nanite assets still need explicit LODs.

Verify with stat scenerendering (watch the triangle count drop as you back away) and the viewport's LOD Coloration view mode, which tints each LOD level a different color so you can see what's swapping where.

Technique 2 — Object pooling

Spawning and destroying actors at runtime is expensive — it allocates memory, runs construction, and creates garbage for the collector. In any game that fires bullets, spawns enemies, or pops particles (your FPS from p3-13, the twin-stick from p3-10, Invaders from p3-6), you should pool them: create a fixed set once, then recycle by toggling visibility/collision instead of destroying.

ProjectilePool.cpp
// Pre-spawn a pool once, hand out inactive ones on demand void AProjectilePool::BeginPlay() { Super::BeginPlay(); for (int32 i = 0; i < PoolSize; ++i) { AProjectile* P = GetWorld()->SpawnActor(ProjectileClass); P->Deactivate(); // hide + disable collision + stop tick Pool.Add(P); } } AProjectile* AProjectilePool::Acquire(const FVector& Loc, const FRotator& Rot) { for (AProjectile* P : Pool) { if (!P->bIsActive) { P->SetActorLocationAndRotation(Loc, Rot); P->Activate(); // reuse — no allocation, no GC return P; } } return nullptr; // pool exhausted — size it for worst case }
Tip.
When a projectile's lifetime ends or it hits something, call Deactivate() to return it to the pool instead of Destroy(). The bullet 'dies' visually but the object lives on, ready to be acquired again.

Technique 3 — Culling

Don't render or tick what the player can't see. UE5 already does frustum culling (skips objects outside the camera) and occlusion culling (skips objects hidden behind others) for rendering. Your job is to help on the gameplay side:

  • Disable Tick when off-screen. An enemy 500 meters away running full AI is wasted CPU. Set SetActorTickEnabled(false) and re-enable when relevant, or use significance-based throttling.
  • Set a Max Draw Distance / Cull Distance Volume. Small props (debris, decals) should vanish past a sensible distance — the player never notices, the GPU saves overdraw.
  • Reduce Tick frequency. Not everything needs to update 60 times a second. Set PrimaryActorTick.TickInterval = 0.2f; to tick a distant manager 5×/sec.
Watch out.
Event Tick in Blueprints is the most common performance killer in student projects. Before optimizing anything fancy, audit every Tick: can it be event-driven instead? A health bar that updates on a 'damage taken' event is free; one that polls health every frame on Tick is pure waste.

A repeatable workflow

  1. Run with stat unit — identify Game vs Draw vs GPU bound.
  2. Capture an Insights trace of a representative slow scene.
  3. Find the single widest bar / biggest stat. Fix only that.
  4. Apply the right tool: LODs (GPU/triangles), pooling (Game/spawning hitches), culling & Tick audits (Game/Draw).
  5. Re-measure. Confirm the number moved. Repeat.
🎮 Build This: Optimization — Profile & Optimize Your Shooter

This is an optimization pass, not a new genre — it extends your 3D FPS from p3-13 (and applies to any prior game). You'll make a measurably faster build.

  1. Open your FPS project and spawn a stress scene: 100+ enemies and a weapon that fires continuously.
  2. Run stat unit in PIE and record Frame/Game/Draw/GPU in ms. Note which is the bottleneck.
  3. Launch with -trace=cpu,gpu,frame, capture a trace, open it in Unreal Insights, and find the widest Game-thread bar in a slow frame.
  4. Replace your bullet SpawnActor/Destroy logic with a pre-spawned ProjectilePool using Acquire/Deactivate.
  5. Add LODs to the enemy mesh (or confirm Nanite) and assign a Cull Distance Volume to small props.
  6. Audit every Event Tick: disable ticking on off-screen enemies and convert at least one polling check into an event.
  7. Re-run stat unit. Document the before/after ms. You should see a clear drop in Game time and frame hitches.

title: Game Feel, Packaging & the Capstone Portfolio
html:
The last 10% is what people remember

You have built real games across nearly every genre — Pong, Breakout, a metroidvania, a 3D FPS, a third-person shooter, a racing game, an RPG, a roguelike, and more. Mechanically they work. But there is a gap between 'works' and 'feels good', and that gap is called game feel (or 'juice'). It's the camera shake when you fire, the brief freeze when a punch lands, the satisfying thump of a sound effect, the little squash-and-stretch on a jump. This final lesson teaches you to add that polish — then to package a real shippable build and assemble your multi-genre capstone portfolio.

Why juice matters.
The same exact mechanic can feel limp or incredible depending entirely on feedback. Juice is cheap to add and disproportionately raises perceived quality. It is the highest return-on-effort work in your whole project.

Camera shake

UE5 ships a Camera Shake system. Create a Blueprint child of UCameraShakeBase (or ULegacyCameraShake for the simple oscillation-based version), set its rotation/location oscillation amplitudes and frequencies, then play it from the player camera manager on impactful events — firing a weapon, taking damage, an explosion.

WeaponComponent.cpp
// Play a camera shake on the local player when the weapon fires void UWeaponComponent::OnFired() { APlayerController* PC = UGameplayStatics::GetPlayerController(this, 0); if (PC && FireShakeClass) { PC->ClientStartCameraShake(FireShakeClass, /*Scale*/ 1.0f); } }

Hit-stop (freeze frames)

Hit-stop is a micro-pause — 50 to 120 ms — applied the instant a hit connects. It sells weight and impact, and is the secret sauce of fighting games and action games. You implement it by briefly slowing global time dilation, then restoring it with a timer.

HitStop.cpp
void ACombatActor::ApplyHitStop(float Duration, float SlowScale) { UWorld* World = GetWorld(); // Slam global time near-zero for a few frames UGameplayStatics::SetGlobalTimeDilation(World, SlowScale); // e.g. 0.05 // Use REAL (unscaled) time so the timer fires despite the slowdown FTimerHandle Handle; World->GetTimerManager().SetTimer(Handle, [World]() { UGameplayStatics::SetGlobalTimeDilation(World, 1.0f); }, Duration, false); }
Watch out.
Global time dilation slows the hit-stop timer too. Either use a custom timer that ignores dilation, or set the duration accounting for the slowdown. A cleaner approach in shipping code is per-actor CustomTimeDilation so only the combatants freeze while UI and camera stay responsive.

Audio and the rest of the juice toolkit

Sound is half of game feel. Use UGameplayStatics::PlaySound2D for UI/non-spatial cues and PlaySoundAtLocation for world sounds. Add small pitch randomization (±10%) so repeated sounds don't feel robotic. Beyond shake/stop/sound, the juice checklist:

  • Particles (Niagara): muzzle flashes, impact sparks, dust on landing — from p3-17.
  • Squash & stretch: scale animations on jump/land/hit for cartoon weight.
  • Screen-space VFX: a damage vignette via post-process, a brief radial blur on a dash.
  • Tweening: never snap UI; ease values in/out with a Timeline or interp.
  • Controller rumble: PlayDynamicForceFeedback on impacts.
Lab.
Take one mechanic from any prior game and apply at least three juice layers (shake + sound + particle). Play it before and after. The difference is the lesson.

Packaging a shippable build

'It runs in the editor' is not a finished game. Packaging cooks your content into a platform-specific, standalone executable. First set your project's defaults in Project Settings → Project → Maps & Modes: pick the Game Default Map (your menu/showcase level) and the default GameMode. Then build from the menu or the command line.

cmd — package for Windows Shipping
# Menu route: Platforms > Windows > Package Project # Set the build configuration to Shipping first (Platforms > ... > Build Configuration) # Command-line route (UnrealAutomationTool): RunUAT.bat BuildCookRun -project="D:/Capstone/Capstone.uproject" \ -platform=Win64 -clientconfig=Shipping \ -cook -build -stage -pak -archive \ -archivedirectory="D:/Builds"

Key flags: -cook converts assets to the platform format, -pak bundles them into compressed .pak files, -stage lays out the runnable folder, and -archive copies the final build to your output directory. Shipping config strips debug code and the console for the smallest, fastest binary. Test the packaged build on a clean machine if you can — packaged behavior can differ from PIE (asset references, default maps, and platform input are common gotchas).

ConfigUse forNotes
DevelopmentTesting buildsHas console + logging
ShippingFinal releaseSmallest, no debug, no console
DebugGameDebugging packaged codeFull symbols, slow
Shipping law.
A game isn't done when it works — it's done when a stranger can download a single archive, double-click it, and play start-to-finish with no editor, no setup, and no crashes.

The capstone portfolio

Your capstone is not one game — it's a showcase of the range you built across this track. Build a small hub menu (UMG, from p3-19) that lets a visitor launch into 3-4 of your best games, each demonstrating a different pillar: a 3D action game (FPS/TPS), the racing game, a 2D game (platformer/metroidvania), and a systems game (RPG/roguelike/sim). Apply the optimization pass from p3-25 and the juice from this lesson to each. Package it once as a single Shipping build.

🎮 Build This: Capstone / Shipping — The Multi-Genre Portfolio

This is the capstone. It extends ALL prior games: you'll polish a curated selection, wrap them in a hub, and ship one packaged build that proves you can build any genre in UE5.

  1. Create a hub level with a UMG main menu that opens into 3-4 of your strongest games via OpenLevel, plus a 'Quit' and 'Back to Hub' option in each.
  2. Add a juice pass to every showcased game: camera shake on impacts, hit-stop on key hits, randomized-pitch sound effects, and at least one Niagara effect.
  3. Apply the p3-25 optimization workflow to each game so the whole portfolio holds a stable frame rate.
  4. In Maps & Modes, set the hub as the Game Default Map; confirm input, audio, and transitions work in Standalone (not just PIE).
  5. Set the build configuration to Shipping and package with BuildCookRun (or the Package Project menu) to an archive folder.
  6. Run the packaged build on a clean machine: it must launch to the hub and let a stranger play through each game with zero setup.
  7. Write a one-paragraph readme per game (genre, what it demonstrates, controls) — that's your portfolio. Congratulations: you can now build any genre in Unreal Engine 5.
